comments:
This paper provides a clear and useful analysis of why linear SVMs trained on HOG features are effective. The main technical contribution is a reformulation that expresses HOG as an affine weighting on the margin of a quadratic-kernel SVM, together with the insight that local second-order pixel interactions are a major source of HOG’s discriminative power. The experiments (natural vs. noise, controlled expression recognition with synthesized geometric perturbations, and a pedestrian detection example) are informative and largely support the central claims: with sufficient capacity and data, local quadratic interactions can reproduce much of HOG’s performance, while simple pixel features cannot.

Strengths: the theoretical reformulation is elegant and connects feature design to classifier capacity/prior in a principled way; the experimental design (controlled warps, scaling of synthesized data) directly probes the paper’s hypotheses; the paper offers a useful perspective for researchers thinking about the role of priors vs. data and capacity.

Weaknesses / suggestions: (1) Contrast normalization is acknowledged but not integrated into the theoretical treatment; its omission weakens full equivalence claims and should be discussed more concretely. (2) Comparisons are limited to pixels and HOG; inclusion of modern learned baselines (e.g. learned convolutional features) or other descriptors would strengthen relevance and situate the results relative to current practice. (3) The reliance on heavy synthetic augmentation and very large datasets is computationally expensive; more analysis of practicality and sample-efficiency (or regularization strategies) would be valuable. (4) Statistical significance and robustness across multiple datasets (beyond faces and one pedestrian set) would increase confidence in generality.

Overall, the paper makes a substantive and well-argued contribution toward understanding a widely used engineering pipeline. I recommend acceptance / broad dissemination after addressing clarifications about contrast normalization, expanding baselines or discussion of modern learned features, and adding more detail about computational cost and practical deployment considerations.
